Justification: The study fits first order multivariate autoregressive models to observed time series of species level catch data from a stationary fish trap and uses these observations to estimate stability metrics. The data are a 25 year series from 1950 to 1974 with catch records aggregated into 12 h intervals and treated as CPUE. Resilience is explicitly defined as return time or return rate to equilibrium from an ecological disturbance or displacement from equilibrium and is governed by the leading eigenvalue of the community matrix. The paper presents three indices of community stability as resistance resilience and reactivity and discusses them together as components of community stability. The target variable used in the resilience calculation is CPUE for three trophic groups which the text treats as an index of abundance. The analysis is at the community level because the resilience metric is derived from a community matrix describing relationships among high predatory meso predatory and low trophic groups. The approach combines field observation for the trap catch time series with modeling and simulation because resilience is quantified from the fitted MAR model and its eigenvalue. The trap is anchored 200 m from the Camogli coast of the Ligurian Sea off the coast of Italy. The first author affiliation is Dalhousie University Halifax NS Canada. Predator depletion is discussed as occurring through fisheries exploitation and fishing effort is identified as the most important predictor of declining community stability. The disturbance is treated as long term predator decline over decades with no defined recovery interval which matches a press disturbance framing. The observation window spans 25 years which is 25 times 365 days.

The study quantifies vegetation resistance and recovery using indices derived from MODIS NDVI time series which are observational remote sensing data. It states that vegetation resilience namely resistance and recovery were quantitatively assessed using indices derived from MODIS NDVI data. Resistance is defined as NDVI 2011 divided by NDVI 2010 and recovery is defined as post drought NDVI in 2012 or 2013 or 2014 divided by NDVI 2011 which makes resilience a combination of resistance and recovery. The target variable used in these indices is NDVI which the paper uses to represent vegetation health and greenness and thus functions as a vegetation index proxy for ecosystem productivity. The study area is the Lower Mississippi River Basin spanning multiple US states and the data are from USDM and MODIS for that region. The first author affiliation is Appalachian State University in the United States. Drought events are described as naturally occurring and the drought severity data are weekly USDM categories so the disturbance is unmanipulated climatic drought. The analysis treats drought as a continuous time window with overlapping drought and recovery periods rather than discrete events which aligns with a press disturbance framing. The observation window for drought events is from March 2 2010 to June 25 2013 and NDVI is extracted for 2010 through 2014 so the first to last measurement spans 2010 to 2014. The riparian systems include floodplains wetlands and bottomland hardwood forests and land cover includes cultivated crops forests and wetlands so the habitat type is multiple. The resilience subject is riparian vegetation which is plants. The location is not given as coordinates so the response uses an approximate basin center coordinate consistent with the named region and reports zero range because only one averaged coordinate is provided.

Framework: ecosystem health = vigor + organization + resilience + stress
Target variable type: Land use; Regional network parameter; Ecosystem function; Abiotic parameter
Justification: The paper develops a VORS regional ecosystem health assessment framework described as Vigor Organization Resilience Stress and gives the formula EHI = EVI×EOI×ERI×ESI. Ecosystem resilience is not measured from recovery dynamics but is computed as ERI = sum Ai×RCi where RCi is an ER coefficient assigned by land use type and taken from previous research with stability and resilience weights. Ecosystem vigor uses annual NDVI and ecosystem organization uses landscape pattern indices from land use data and Fragstats and ecosystem stress uses land use intensity soil erosion intensity and rocky desertification intensity. These are proxy and index based components rather than observed post disturbance recovery trajectories so resilience measurement is inferred. The study uses RS and GIS datasets including MOD13A1 NDVI and land use and other spatial layers for Guizhou Province from 2000 to 2020 which is field observation via remote sensing and existing datasets. The study area is Guizhou Province in southwest China with coordinates 24•37′-29•13′N and 103•36′-109•35′E and the affiliated institution is Guizhou University in China. No explicit disturbance event is imposed or tracked for recovery and the analysis focuses on ecosystem health indices and drivers rather than resilience response to a defined disturbance.

The study defines interaction network resilience as the ability of interacting organisms to maintain their functions processes or populations after experiencing a disturbance. The study calculates bee and plant network resilience using the network resilience parameter βeff described as a one dimensional mathematical model quantifiable from an ecological network and defining the state of an ecosystem against disturbances such as loss in interactions species interaction weight or combinations therefrom. Resilience is therefore quantified from a proxy network metric rather than from observed recovery dynamics of a state variable through time. The methods describe field surveys of bee species and their interactions with plants across four seasons on 50 study plots along two elevation gradients in Kenya. The resilience target is the interaction network property βeff derived from the plant bee interaction matrix and thus the target variable type is interaction. Because βeff is computed for plant and bee assemblages within plots it reflects a community level property. The approach is field observation because plant bee interactions were recorded by observers in plots. The first author affiliation list includes International Centre of Insect Physiology and Ecology in Nairobi Kenya. The study area coordinates are given for Taita Hills and Murang’a County and the average of the midpoints is approximately S1.09 E37.36. The farthest midpoint differs from the average by about 1.03 degrees latitude and 1.07 degrees longitude giving a summed range of about 2.1. The plots include savannah shrubland indigenous bushland pasture human settlement and subsistence farming and plantations so multiple habitat types are involved. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are NA because the measurement is inferred.

The study used experimental trampling treatments and measured vegetation response immediately before trampling then about 2 weeks after trampling and 1 year after trampling. The response variable used to build indices was relative vegetation cover defined as the fraction of original vegetation that survives trampling adjusted for changes on controls. Resilience is defined as the ability of a vegetation type to recover following the cessation of trampling and the resilience index is the change in relative vegetation cover during the year after trampling expressed as a fraction of the change in cover that occurred 2 weeks after trampling. This is a direct quantification from observed cover change after a disturbance so the measurement type is observed. The paper presents three indices resistance tolerance and resilience as components used to express vegetation response. Because resilience is quantified as the amount of cover regained relative to the initial cover loss it matches recovery degree rather than recovery rate or recovery time. The target variable used for resilience quantification is vegetation cover. Cover is summed across species for vegetation types and also calculated for individual species so the level is community. The approach is a field experiment because controlled trampling was applied in lanes at 18 study sites in five mountainous regions of the United States. The first author affiliation is the Aldo Leopold Wilderness Research Institute Forest Service US Department of Agriculture in Missoula Montana. The field country is the United States because sites were in Washington Montana Colorado New Hampshire and North Carolina. Specific coordinates are not provided in the text tables or figures so location and location range are NA. Multiple vegetation types across several regions and both forested and non forested settings are included so habitat type is multi. The subjects are vascular plant species plus mosses and lichens but resilience indices are reported for vegetation types and plant species so the taxon is plantae. The disturbance is manipulated trampling by people walking passes along lanes so it is manipulated biotic. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event with follow up recovery so the pattern is pulse. The observation duration from initial measurement before trampling to the 1 year measurement is 365 days.

The study states it uses the Vigor-Organization-Resilience model and that ecosystem health is evaluated through vigor organization and resilience which makes resilience a component within ecosystem health. Resilience is computed as ER = sum Ai × RCi where RCi is an ecosystem elasticity coefficient derived by combining expert scores and previous studies and Ai is land use area which indicates resilience is inferred from land use and expert based coefficients rather than measured recovery dynamics. The methods define resilience conceptually as ability to recover after disturbances but the operational formula uses land use areas and coefficients not time series recovery. The paper evaluates ecosystem health primarily from the perspective of tourism land use and uses land use data remote sensing images and POI points to delineate tourism land types. NDVI is used for vigor and landscape indices for organization but resilience itself is based on land use type areas and expert derived coefficients. The study area is Jiaozhou Bay in Qingdao Shandong Province China and the coastal zone is townships within 10 km of the coastline. The first author affiliation is Ocean University of China in Qingdao China. The figures and text locate Jiaozhou Bay on the southern coast of the Jiaodong Peninsula in the Yellow Sea and an approximate central coordinate is provided for Qingdao Jiaozhou Bay. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration for recovery are not applicable.

The introduction defines resilience as a function of resistance and recovery with the phrase resilience can be considered as a function of resistance and recovery. The methods compile observational data of total coral cover before and after acute storms temperature stress and coastal run off from flooding events. Disturbance impact is quantified as the log response ratio of pre to post disturbance coral cover. Recovery is quantified as an annual linear recovery rate in an approximate 5 year time window following disturbance by fitting linear models of coral cover versus years since disturbance and using the slope as recovery rate. Because resilience is explicitly framed as resistance and recovery and the core recovery analysis quantifies resilience using recovery rate the quantification is recovery rate. The target variable used for both impact and recovery analyses is percentage hard coral cover. Total coral cover aggregates across scleractinian hard corals so the level is community. The approach is field observation because the study extracts empirical coral cover measurements from published field surveys and does not impose experimental manipulations. The first author affiliation is The University of Western Australia in Australia. The location is tropical coral reefs globally and the results state the database spans 54 degrees of latitude from 26 S to 28 N so the field country and location are global. The habitat is coral reefs in coastal and shelf waters which corresponds to the marine neritic zone. The major taxa studied are scleractinian hard corals which are animals. Disturbances are acute storms and temperature stress events and flood plume run off which are treated as discrete events with pre and post measurements and a recovery window so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The recovery rate window is 5 years following disturbance so the observation duration is 1825 days.

The study fits state space models to daily gross primary production time series from six rivers and explicitly models flood disturbance effects on latent autotrophic biomass using daily discharge. Resilience is quantified as the low density maximum growth rate rmax because the Methods state they estimated resilience as the median value of the maximum growth rate rmax and calculated daily maximum percent increase in biomass as (ermax−1)×100. This is a recovery rate quantification because rmax is a daily growth rate and the paper describes it as fast recovery and as recovery rates and doubling times. The target variable used to quantify resilience is latent photosynthetically active autotrophic biomass Bt because rmax is a parameter of the biomass process model. The approach is modeling and simulation because they developed and fit the latent biomass time series model in Stan using Bayesian inference and used out of sample prediction. The disturbance is unmanipulated hydrological because the disturbance is high flows and floods observed in river discharge time series and described as flood disturbances and high flows. The disturbance pattern is pulse because they describe discrete high flow events such as high flows on 8 May 2013 and recovery following the disturbance over about a week. Observation duration is 730 days because they used 2 year time series and fit the first year and predicted the second year. The first author affiliation is University of Nevada Reno in the United States. The field country is the United States because the rivers are in US states including WV MD GA OK CA TX and OR. Habitat type is wetland because the data are from streams and rivers and the study is river ecosystem productivity. Taxon is algae because the paper repeatedly refers to algal biomass and autotrophic organisms such as algae and periphyton as the primary producers whose biomass resilience is assessed.

The study imposed simulated spates in outdoor experimental stream channels and sampled communities immediately after each spate and at subsequent 3 d intervals with a final spate on day 33. The Introduction states resistance was measured as change in algal biomass and taxonomic structure across a disturbance and resilience was assessed as convergence with a temporally changing undisturbed control community. The Methods state resilience was assessed by comparing rates of cell accrual on control and disturbed tiles during the 9 d following each simulated spate and resilience in diatom community composition was examined by comparing trajectories on DCA ordination plots. The Results state resilience was higher in slow current with disturbed communities reaching biomass and taxonomic structure similar to controls after 3 to 9 d which provides a recovery time. The target variables used for resilience are cell densities and diatom assemblage composition which correspond to abundance and composition at the community level. The work was conducted at the University of Louisville experimental stream facility in Bernheim Forest Nature Preserve Bullitt County Kentucky USA and the facility draws water from Harts Run draining a basin of mixed hardwood forest which supports Forest habitat. The first author affiliation is University of Louisville Louisville Kentucky USA which is in the United States. The disturbance is a simulated spate created by increasing current and scouring tiles with a high pressure hose which is a manipulated hydrological pulse. The experiment ran from day 0 to day 33 so the observation duration is 33 days.

The paper defines ecological resilience as the ability of a system to absorb changes to maintain identity and states that functional evenness has been used as a proxy for the resilience of communities. The study quantifies functional evenness and other diversity metrics and uses them to compare invaded and non-invaded bird communities and to relate diversity to land use and heterogeneity. Because resilience is treated via a proxy metric rather than direct recovery dynamics after a disturbance it is inferred. The introduction presents resilience alongside resistance and states that a resilient system should better resist disturbance events and it also defines community resistance as the capacity to withstand disturbance. The target variables used in the main analyses are diversity metrics including species richness functional divergence functional evenness and mean pairwise phylogenetic distance. These metrics are calculated for bird communities at survey points so the level is community and the taxon is Animalia. The approach is field observation because bird surveys were conducted using 10 min point counts at 189 points visited in 2021 to 2022. The study area is the Tagus estuary area Portugal and the first author affiliation is University of Lisbon Portugal. The map and text locate the study around the Tagus Estuary near Lisbon so the coordinates are approximated to that area. The landscape includes forest agriculture urban shrubland and wetland so the habitat type is multi. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience are NA because the measurement is inferred and the study does not quantify recovery following a defined disturbance event.

The study states it developed an ecological resilience assessment process chain across the disturbance carrying recovery dimensions and names it the D-C-R three-dimensional process model chain. Ecological resilience is quantified as an index ERI computed from weighted components EDF ECC and ERC with ERI = ωdi EDF + ωci ECC + ωri ERC. EDF ECC and ERC are constructed from multiple indicators including topography climate hydrology land activities economic development landscape pattern ecosystem services vegetation indices and socio-economic indicators rather than direct recovery trajectories of a single state variable. The methods describe weighted calculations and normalization to build an ER atlas library and analyze spatiotemporal patterns from 2000 to 2020. Because resilience is derived from indicator systems and composite weighting rather than direct observation of system recovery after a defined disturbance the measurement is inferred. The target variable used to quantify resilience is the composite ER index mapped on 1 km by 1 km grids which is a spatially derived index. The study area is the Dongting Lake region spanning Hunan and Hubei provinces and the case cities are Yueyang Changde Yiyang and Jingzhou. The first author affiliation is Central South University in Changsha China. The data sources include remote sensing and other spatial datasets processed in ArcGIS indicating field observation via remote sensing and spatial analysis. The paper does not quantify resilience as recovery time rate degree resistance latitude or invariability but as a composite index so quantification is inferred. The habitat context includes lake waters floodplains wetlands forests cultivated lands and built areas so multiple habitat types are involved. No focal organism taxon is assessed because the resilience metric is an ecosystem landscape index. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration for recovery are not specified as imposed or observed disturbance response metrics.

The study quantified resilience and resistance from NDVI time series using an ARx model with NDVI from 2001 to 2022 and hydroclimatic anomalies. The Methods state that NDVI anomaly and hydroclimatic indicators were decomposed and anomaly terms were input to the ARx model and the coefficient alpha characterizes resilience. The Methods explain that a larger absolute value of alpha implies a slow rate of returning to an equilibrium state from a disturbance which is a recovery rate concept. The Introduction states resistance and resilience are the two most fundamental aspects of ecosystem stability and defines resilience as the ability to recover its original state after disturbances. The framework is explicitly stated as resistance and resilience being aspects of ecosystem stability. The target variable used in the resilience quantification is NDVI which the paper uses as an indicator to reflect changes in ecosystem status and ecosystem characteristics which is treated here as an ecosystem function proxy. The study area is the arid region of Northwest China with coordinates 73E to 107E and 34N to 49N and the average coordinate is used for Location with range computed from the bounding box. The first author affiliation is Lanzhou University China. Disturbances are hydroclimatic anomalies including precipitation soil moisture and potential evapotranspiration treated as external disturbances and analyzed as anomalies over the full period which corresponds to a press type driver. The observation window spans 2001 to 2022 which is 22 years giving 8030 days using 365 days per year.

The introduction states that the study considers engineering resilience as the return time of individual plant species in a community to the equilibrium after abrupt change events. The methods describe process based model simulation experiments with a 50 year burn in period then conditions changed for one year then returned to the previous level for 100 simulation years. The methods state that return time was determined as the number of years for which control and treatment runs differed significantly after the change event. The methods also describe resistance as no response and discuss that SDMs may underestimate resistance and overestimate resilience. The disturbance is explicitly imposed in the model as abrupt changes of mean annual groundwater level and salinity from one year to the next for one year. The response variable used for PBM occurrence is species specific relative frequency defined as the proportion of runs in which a given species was present. The study site for the illustrative example is Michaelsdorf Germany with coordinates 12.56 E and 54.36 N. The first author affiliation is Technische Universitaet Braunschweig Germany. The discussion explicitly contrasts resistance and resilience and notes that Scirpus maritimus focuses on resistance rather than resilience. The observation window for the PBM experiment spans 50 plus 1 plus 100 simulation years which equals 151 years.

The introduction defines resilience as rate of recovery by stating greater resilience is rate of recovery. The methods describe a 27 month field experiment assessing algal recruitment and succession in cleared patches that mimic naturally forming gaps which is a controlled disturbance. Recovery was quantified from time series surveys because patches were surveyed monthly and perennial algal cover increased over time until reaching ambient cover. The statistical analysis states to analyze recovery rates they compared the slope of perennial algal cover over time among treatments and examined the interaction between time and treatments to assess the effect of treatments on recovery rates. This makes the measurement observed and the quantification recovery rate. The target variable type is cover because recovery rates were based on perennial algal cover over time and figures show time series of cover. The level is community because cover and diversity were measured for multiple algal species in patches. The approach is field experiment because they conducted a field experiment in the rocky intertidal zone. The institution country is US because the first author affiliation is University of California Davis California USA. The field country is US and the location is Bodega Marine Reserve northern coast of California USA with coordinates 3881901200N 123 8402400W. The habitat type is coastal because the study is in a rocky intertidal zone. The taxon is Plantae because the focal organisms are macroalgal species and seaweed. Disturbance includes manipulated structural clearing of patches and manipulated biotic herbivore reduction using barriers and removal of mobile invertebrates. The disturbance pattern is pulse because patches were cleared in May 2008 as a discrete event and then recovery was observed. Observation duration is 810 days because the experiment lasted 27 months.

The introduction states that anticipating resilience requires understanding population responses to recurrent disturbances and the study focuses on subtropical and tropical coral assemblages. The methods describe annual surveys of 3171 tagged coral colonies across four regions in eastern Australia and southern Japan between 2016 and 2019 and these are direct observational demographic data. The study then uses integral projection models parameterized with observed survival transitions fragmentation fecundity and recruitment as functions of colony size to quantify population characteristics. The methods explicitly define short-term potential as characteristics following displacement from stationary equilibrium due to disturbances and define the damping ratio as a measure of demographic recovery describing the rate at which a perturbed population converges back to its asymptotic growth trajectory. This makes the resilience measurement observed and quantified via a recovery rate type metric. Resilience is presented through short-term demographic characteristics including demographic recovery and short-term potential and the discussion calls this a framework for quantifying demographic resilience. The target variables used for resilience quantification are demographic parameters derived from the IPMs including damping ratio and transient envelope. The level is population because the IPMs and resulting metrics describe population growth and recovery trajectories. The approach combines field observation with modeling and simulation because field surveys parameterize IPMs and resilience metrics are computed from the models. The first author affiliation is University of St Andrews in the United Kingdom. Field data are from Australia and Japan and the figure text specifies Okinawa and Kochi in Japan and Heron Island and Solitary Islands Marine Park in Australia with GPS coordinates given for AS AT JS and JT. Averaging the provided GPS coordinates yields approximately S1.1 E140.0 and the farthest site differs by about 14.3 degrees when summing maximum latitude and longitude deviations. The habitat is shallow coral reef communities and marine parks which fits Marine.Neritic. The study subjects are corals which are animals. Abiotic variability is represented by sea surface temperature regimes and the study links short-term dynamics to recurrent disturbance in stochastic environments so the disturbance is unmanipulated climatic variability treated as a continuous regime and thus press. The surveys span from 2016 to 2019 which is 3 years or 1095 days.

The methods state that they aimed "to approximate the resilience of each lineage in P. farinosa to climate change" by modeling "current and future distribution of suitable habitats expressed as the number of climatically suitable cells" using SDMs and future climate projections. This treats resilience as predicted persistence of suitable habitat rather than measuring recovery dynamics of the system itself. The study quantifies intraspecific "genomic diversity" using "expected heterozygosity" and "allelic richness" and quantifies ecological diversity using "climatic niche breadth" and niche similarity and then relates these to predicted future suitable habitat. Because resilience is evaluated through proxy indicators and modeled habitat suitability rather than observed recovery after a disturbance it is classified as inferred. No explicit equation framework defining resilience is provided in the introduction methods or discussion. The first author affiliation is "Univ. of Zurich Zurich Switzerland" which is CH. Occurrence and sampling are across Europe with regions named as "CW Alps" "W Carpathians" "Scandinavia" and "Pyrenees" and the introduction specifies distribution in "Carpathians Alps Iberia" and "British Isles Baltic region and southern Scandinavia" so multiple European countries are involved but coordinates are not provided. Habitat is described as "calcareous fens edges of streams or lakes and drier calcareous grasslands" and the major explicitly named habitat type is calcareous fens which are wetlands. The focal organism is the plant "Primula farinosa".

The paper states that they created a nonlinear model for lake chlorophyll and that they simulated a population of lakes to obtain the distribution of chlorophyll and resilience across lakes. Resilience is defined as the capacity of a system to remain within a stability domain or state and the size of the region between the desired stable state and the separatrix represents the resilience of the stable state. In Methods they state that resilience of the clear-water state is the size of the area between the lower stable equilibrium and the unstable equilibrium and that stochastic shocks that push the system across the unstable boundary will result in a system within the basin of attraction of the upper equilibrium value. This is a threshold based definition so the quantification matches latitude because it is the magnitude of disturbance a system can withstand without shifting to an alternative stable state. The response variable used in the model is areal chlorophyll and chlorophyll is treated as a lake level state variable so the target variable type is ecosystem function and the level is ecosystem. The approach is modeling and simulation because they solved the model for equilibria and ran Monte Carlo simulations for 1000 trials and also simulated 150000 lakes. The first author affiliation line gives Center for Limnology University of Wisconsin Madison Wisconsin USA so the institution country is US. Driver and calibration data include northern Wisconsin lakes and experimental lakes in northern Michigan and a DOC regression for small lakes in Ontario Canada so field countries include US and CA. The system is lakes and the text links DOC variation to wetland area and the study is about lakes so the habitat type is wetland. The focal state variable is chlorophyll which represents phytoplankton so the taxon is Chromista. Disturbance is represented in the model by changes in phosphorus loading and by angler harvest propagated through the food web so disturbance types are model resource and model biotic. The disturbances are treated as maintained driver levels in equilibrium simulations rather than discrete events so the disturbance pattern is press. No explicit time span between first and last measurements for resilience observation is provided because resilience is computed from equilibria so observation duration is NA.

The study defines high environmental quality urban areas partly by avian communities with high potential resilience to face ecological stress. The Methods state that potential resilience of bird assemblages was estimated by combining species richness and redundancy of functional traits. The potential community resilience was computed as a discrete classification based on species richness and the functional evenness FEve index. This uses proxy indicators rather than measuring recovery dynamics after a disturbance so the measurement type is inferred. The Introduction defines ecological resilience as capacity to absorb environmental changes without altering main functions but the Methods do not quantify return time return rate or return degree after a disturbance. The target variables used to quantify resilience are species richness and FEve which correspond to diversity and a functional trait distribution metric. Bird data were collected by standardised point counts in fifteen European cities during the 2018 breeding season which is field observation. The corresponding author affiliation is Czech University of Life Sciences Prague in the Czech Republic. The study area includes cities in ten European countries listed in Table 1. The Methods say the location of each point was recorded with a GPS but no coordinates are provided in the text. The study focuses on urban areas and urbanized gradients so the habitat type is urban. The resilience proxy is based on avian communities so the taxon is Animalia and the level is community.

The paper proposes a quantitative measure called the Persistence Index to assess the capacity of communities to maintain their functions and services after disturbances and it is formulated from response trait richness and response trait abundance using a species specific response trait matrix and species relative abundances. The index is based on the assumption that an ecosystem will be more resilient and resistant if it contains a greater presence of species with a given set of response traits and it does not quantify recovery dynamics of an observed ecosystem state variable after disturbance. The study discusses resilience and resistance as complementary concepts and states that resilience is defined as the ability to undergo disturbance without shifting to an alternative state while resistance relates to the amount of external pressure needed to bring about a given amount of disturbance. Resilience is operationalized through trait based proxies including the number of response traits present the abundance of species presenting response traits and redundancy of response traits across species. The case study applies the index to tree communities across forests in Peninsular Spain and the Balearic Islands using the Third Spanish National Forest Inventory plots. The approach includes Monte Carlo simulations and an empirical calculation using national forest inventory data which is field observation. The first author affiliation is Forest Sciences Centre of Catalonia in Solsona Spain. The disturbances considered for trait selection and weighting are wildfire drought and windstorm events but because resilience measurement is inferred from traits the disturbance type pattern and observation duration for recovery are not applicable. The target variables used to quantify the index are response traits and their redundancy and abundance which are interaction network related proxies rather than direct measures of cover biomass or productivity recovery.

Framework: resilience = distance to the boundary of the attraction basin
Justification: The paper states that it mathematically defines resilience as the distance to the boundary of the attraction basin and repeatedly frames this as distance to tipping points. It develops a one dimensional function and a scaling factor to compare distances to tipping points across systems and it reports that it compares distances to tipping points across different systems on the basis of data on abundance and topology. The abundance variable is explicitly Pi and Ai and the reduced variable x captures the mean field abundance and is described as weighted average biomass of all species using the reduction approach. The study uses simulations that impose perturbations on links and nodes and it also applies state perturbations and monitors recovery rate and autocorrelation in simulated dynamics. The Methods describe parameter estimation by simulating species abundance and matching proportional abundances to real ones and then using those parameters to obtain tipping points. The disturbance manipulations include removing or adding links and nodes and changing weights and applying state perturbations described as a sudden reduction by a factor of 0.95. The disturbance events are applied at steps and as sudden reductions which matches a pulse treatment. The approach is therefore modeling and simulation rather than direct field observation because the resilience quantification is obtained from simulated dynamics and reduced equations even though empirical network and abundance snapshots are used for parameterization. The first author affiliation is Shanghai Jiao Tong University in China. The field data are from pollination networks and abundance data collected in seven previous papers including locations explicitly named as Nahuel Huapi National Park in Argentina and Morne Seychellois National Park Mahé and Hickling and Tenerife and Chiloé Island and Thailand and the paper also refers to Chesapeake Bay in the USA as an example but not as a data source for the 54 networks. The paper does not provide explicit coordinates in the provided text because the coordinates are said to be in Extended Data Fig 1 but the numeric values are not included here. The habitat context spans multiple locations and includes at least temperate forest and sub alpine desert and heathland and national parks so a single dominant IUCN habitat type cannot be determined from the provided text. The abundance data are stated to be for plant species and the networks are plant pollinator mutualistic networks so the taxon for the quantified abundance includes Plantae.

The abstract states that sea-level rise thresholds facilitate evaluation of ecosystem resilience and the study derives thresholds for salt marsh stability under sea-level rise. The Methods state that a mechanistic model predicted salt marsh change at the Grand Bay National Estuarine Research Reserve in Mississippi United States for 2050 and 2100 under sea-level rise rates from 4 to 20 mm per year and that beyond thresholds salt marshes would transition to a less desirable state with much smaller and fragmented salt marsh landscape. Thresholds were derived by fitting a sigmoidal regression of each landscape metric or the first principal component versus sea-level rise rate and taking the inflection point as the threshold. This is a limit beyond which the system shifts toward an alternative state so the resilience quantification matches latitude. Resilience is quantified using landscape metrics and principal components describing spatial patterns of salt marsh distributions rather than recovery dynamics so the target variable type is composition. The approach is modeling and simulation because salt marsh distributions and landscape metrics were predicted from a mechanistic wetland change model and compared with a neutral model. The first author affiliation is The University of Southern Mississippi in the United States. The study area section gives Grand Bay NERR at 30.37 N 88.43 W in Mississippi United States. The disturbance is sea-level rise imposed as scenarios in the model so it is model climatic and it is treated as a continuous rate driver across the simulation window so it is press. The simulation spans from the initial wetland map based on 1988 NWI data to target years 2100 so the observation duration is 2100 minus 1988 years converted to days.

The study states that it constructed a Drought Vulnerability Index integrating exposure sensitivity and resilience as three core dimensions defined in the IPCC framework and it defines VI as integrating EI SI and RI. Sensitivity and resilience were derived from autoregressive modeling of vegetation dynamics using vegetation and climate data from 2001 to 2023. Vegetation condition was assessed using monthly NDVI from MODIS and drought exposure used monthly PDSI from TerraClimate. The AR model specifies NDVIt as a function of concurrent PDSI temperature and lagged NDVI and it states that δ serves as an indirect measure of ecosystem resilience and that larger δ suggests slower recovery and therefore lower resilience and thus RI is expressed as 1 minus δ. Because δ is an autoregressive coefficient describing temporal dependency and slower recovery it corresponds to recovery rate type quantification. The target variable used in the resilience model is NDVI which is a vegetation index used to represent vegetation condition and dynamics and is treated as an ecosystem level function proxy. The disturbance is drought described as a climatic hazard and it is not experimentally manipulated so it is unmanipulated climatic. Drought is characterized as prolonged imbalance lasting from several months to multiple years and the analysis uses continuous monthly time series so the disturbance is treated as press. The study area overview gives the Yangtze River Basin coordinate bounds 28 N to 35 N and 90 E to 122 E and the average is reported. The farthest corner from the average differs by 3.5 degrees latitude and 16 degrees longitude giving 19.5 total and the maximum relative difference sum is reported as 17.5 based on the average used. The vegetation types include forests shrublands savannas grasslands wetlands and croplands so the habitat type is multi. The first author affiliation is Nantong University in China. The observation window is from 2001 to 2023 using monthly data which spans 22 years giving 8030 days using 365 days per year.

Framework: ecosystem health = vigor + organization + resilience + services
Justification: The study states it quantitatively assessed ecosystem health based on the Vigor – Organization – Resilience – Services VORS model which defines a framework where ecosystem health includes resilience. Ecosystem resilience is not measured as recovery dynamics but is calculated as a coefficient RCi using NDVI and land use class restoring force coefficients with fixed values for grassland building land cropland forestland water and unutilized land. The text says the closer the region land use is to that of a natural ecosystem the greater its resilience indicating resilience is inferred from land use and NDVI based coefficients rather than observed recovery after a disturbance. The data sources include NDVI landcover and other spatial layers and the analysis extracts values into 3 km by 3 km grids indicating a landscape scale assessment. The study area is Fujian Province in southeastern China and the first author affiliations are in China. No explicit disturbance event is imposed or analyzed for recovery and no coordinates are provided beyond the province level and a location figure.

The study states that it measured success using the ability of restored vegetation to recover from disturbance and it tested resilience by simulating a physical disturbance through clipping of all above ground cordgrass biomass and removal of mussels. It then measured cordgrass survival and regrowth by counting the number of shoots that emerged per transplant 10 days and 13 months following the simulated disturbance. These are direct measurements of system response after a disturbance so the measurement type is observed. No explicit equation or decomposition defining resilience as part of a larger framework is provided so the framework is none. Resilience is quantified using survival percentages of plots still harbouring vegetation and shoot regrowth counts after disturbance which represent recovery degree and recovery rate. The target variables used for resilience are survival and shoot regrowth which correspond to demography parameter and growth. These measures are for cordgrass transplants within plots so the level is population. The work is described as a 29 month full factorial field experiment in a southeastern US saltmarsh. The study site is on Sapelo Island Georgia USA at latitude 31.40749167 and longitude minus 81.28987601. The first author affiliation is Radboud University Nijmegen the Netherlands. The habitat is a saltmarsh coastal wetland. The focal organism for resilience is Spartina alterniflora which is Plantae. The disturbance is experimentally imposed clipping and removal so it is manipulated structural and treated as a discrete event followed by recovery measurements so it is pulse. The observation window for resilience spans from the disturbance in August 2014 to 13 months later which is 10 days plus 13 months or 405 days and the full experiment duration from April 2013 to 13 months after August 2014 is 29 months or 875 days.

Framework: community stability = sensitivity + asymmetry + resistance + resilience
Justification: The study used long term observational and consecutive ANPP data at least 10 years and resampled yearly ANPP within nested time windows. Extreme climatic events were classified as extreme drought years and extreme wet years using SPEI and their frequency was calculated as the count of years in which ECEs occurred. Resistance and resilience were calculated following Isbell et al. 2015 using Yn as average ANPP across all non ECE years and Ye and Ye+1 as ANPP during an ECE year and a year following an ECE. The paper defines resilience as the level of productivity relative to its undisturbed state after an ECE and quantifies it with Ye Ye+1 and Yn which is a post event recovery relative to the undisturbed state. The intermediate processes of sensitivity asymmetry resistance and resilience are explicitly analysed as associated processes for ANPP stability which is described as community stability. The target variable used to quantify resilience is ANPP which is aboveground net primary productivity and is treated as community productivity. The data are from natural grassland sites including alpine grassland on the Tibetan Plateau the Eurasian steppe and the North American prairie. The location is described as twenty two sites in Asia and fourteen sites in North America and specific coordinates are not provided in the text. The first author affiliation is Lanzhou University Lanzhou China. The disturbance is unmanipulated climatic because ECEs are extreme drought or wet events derived from climate station data and not experimentally imposed. The disturbance is treated as discrete events because ECEs are classified by years and resilience uses the year following an ECE. The time period is 1980s to 2010s which corresponds to about 30 years for the observation window.

The study used a long-term vegetation record from the Sonoran Desert with plant species cover measured 26 times between 1953 and 2009 in 68 permanent transects. It quantified community transitions using changes in species composition between successive samplings and reported transition frequency and compositional dissimilarity. Table 1 states that diagonal entries represent the percentage of occasions where a sample did not undergo transition and indicate resilience which operationalizes resilience as remaining unchanged. This corresponds to resistance because it reflects the ability to deviate less from the original community state between sampling times. The target variable used to quantify this is species composition because transitions were defined as reassignment due to changes in species composition and dissimilarity was calculated with Bray Curtis. The work is field observation because it analyzed repeated field measurements on permanent transects and notes transects did not experience direct vegetation manipulations. The disturbance driver analyzed with transitions was precipitation fluctuation with transitions and dissimilarity positively correlated with precipitation and the transects were not manipulated. The disturbance is treated as press because precipitation variation is analyzed as an ongoing driver across the 1957 to 2009 record without a defined recovery interval. The first author affiliation is Texas A and M University in the United States. The field site is the Santa Rita Experimental Range in Arizona United States in the Sonoran Desert near Tucson. The habitat is desert because the study explicitly describes the Sonoran Desert landscape and semiarid conditions. The taxa are plants because resilience metrics are based on cover of 22 plant species. The observation duration spans from 1953 to 2009 which is 56 years or 20440 days.

The paper states that it uses an extended Klausmeier reaction diffusion model and that it employs numerical simulations with a(t)=0.5(1-t/T) with T=10^5 or T=10^4 which is an explicitly imposed change in rainfall. Box 3 defines ecological resilience as the amount of environmental change a system can withstand before it flips to another regime and specifies it as the difference between current rainfall a and the critical value ac,N that is a-ac,N. This is a threshold based quantification so it matches Latitude. The disturbance is climatic because the imposed driver is decreasing rainfall and it is model imposed so it is Model_Climatic. The disturbance is treated as continuous change in a over the simulation window so it is Press. The target variable used in the resilience quantification in Box 3 is rainfall a and ac,N so the target variable type is Abiotic parameter. The study system is vegetation patches and biomass in semi arid dryland ecosystems so the taxon is Plantae and the habitat is dryland desertification context so Desert. The first author affiliation is Leiden University in the Netherlands so institution country is NL. The data accessibility statement says no new data were used so field country and location are NA. The simulation duration is given by T in a(t) and the slow climate change simulation uses T=10^5 time steps so observation duration is 100000.

Framework: UEH = pressure + vigor + organization + resilience + service
Justification: The paper proposes the Pressure-Vigor-Organization-Resilience-Service modeling framework and states that UEH is the target layer which consists of five criterion layers P V O R S. Resilience is quantified as habitat quality HQ within the indicator system and UEHI is computed by aggregating normalized indicators with weights. Resilience is not measured as recovery after a disturbance and is instead represented by an indicator HQ derived from land use and threat factors so resilience measurement is inferred. The study relies on Landsat images DEM geographic information and socio-economic data and uses random forest land use classification and indicator calculations in ArcGIS and Fragstats which is field observation using remote sensing and spatial datasets. The first author affiliation is Fujian Normal University in Fuzhou China so the institution country is CN. The study areas are Changle in China and Suez in Egypt so the field countries are CN and EG. Coordinates are provided as ranges for Changle and Suez and the average of the midpoints is N27.93 E76.61. The analysis is an urban ecosystem health assessment for coastal cities and uses land use classification as the fundamental basis for computing UEHI so the target variable type is land use and the level is landscape. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable.

The introduction defines ecological resilience as the degree and rate of recovery of a community from a perturbation. The methods state they experimentally disturbed grasslands by digging and removing plant biomass including roots and shoots. The methods state they quantified recovery of the native plant communities resilience and estimated cover and species richness of all plant taxa to assess community responses to disturbance. The methods specify the measure of community recovery as the degree to which native cover and richness metrics in disturbed plots matched levels measured in adjacent undisturbed plots in each post treatment year. The results report resilience to disturbance measured as recovery of native cover and richness in unseeded plots in the 2 years following treatment. The results state native plant cover recovered to undisturbed levels in La Pampa by year 2 indicating very high resilience. The first author affiliation is Rocky Mountain Research Station United States Forest Service Missoula Montana USA. The study areas section provides centroid coordinates for Turkey Montana and La Pampa. The study areas are described as natural bunchgrass communities grassland sites. Treatments were initiated at the end of the growing seasons and cover was estimated pretreatment and in the second post treatment year for plots initiated in year 1 indicating a two year post treatment window.

The paper is a concepts and synthesis study that uses stochastic differential equation models and simulations including Eq. 3 Eq. 8 and Eq. 15 and shows realizations in Fig. 1b Fig. 2c and Fig. 2d. It treats perturbations as continual stochastic noise because it states that the ball is subject to continual stochastically varying perturbations and that noise occurs continually with low intensity. It quantifies stability using potential and quasi-potential surfaces and explicitly links Holling resilience to basin height and states that Holling defines resilience as the height of the lowest point of the basin of attraction. It also states that Hodgson et al. include latitude as the width of the basin of attraction and elasticity as the asymptotic stability metric and it presents basin width as a stability metric in Fig. 5a. Because the requested output is for resilience and the text defines resilience as basin height and also discusses latitude as a component of resilience the quantification is coded as latitude as the basin width metric. The target variables in the core models are state variables including nutrient concentration and consumer resource densities and these are population interaction dynamics so the target variable type is interaction and the level is community because the consumer resource and predator prey models involve more than one species. The first author affiliation is Case Western Reserve University Cleveland OH USA so the institution country is US. No empirical field data collection country or coordinates are provided because the study analyzes classic models and simulations rather than field sampling. The disturbance is modeled as stochastic perturbations to inputs and state variables including random nutrient input and additive noise terms so it is coded as model resource disturbance with a press pattern. The longest explicit simulation window shown is the realization over time interval 0 to 50 in Fig. 2d so the observation duration is 50 days as the only stated duration.

Framework: ecological adaptability cycle = potential + resilience + connectivity
Justification: The study applies a three-dimensional framework described as the ecological adaptability cycle with the components potential resilience connectivity and it states this framework is used to extract ecological source. Resilience is not quantified as recovery of a system state after a defined disturbance but is treated as an indicator where continuous green trend of vegetation indicates stronger resilience and pixels with significantly increased NDVI are used as resilient pixels. This makes resilience an inferred property derived from the NDVI change trend rather than a measured recovery trajectory after a disturbance event. Because measurement is inferred quantification is inferred. The resilience related proxy is NDVI change trend and NDVI is used here as a vegetation productivity related index within ecosystem services and vegetation restoration context so the target variable type is ecosystem function. The resilience proxy is calculated for vegetation cover dynamics across patches so the level aligns with ecosystem scale rather than individual population or community. Data sources include MODIS NDVI and other spatial datasets processed for the Yanhe River Basin so the approach is field observation using remote sensing and GIS layers. The first author affiliation is Xi’an University of Science and Technology in China so institution country is CN. The study area is the Yanhe River Basin in China with coordinates 36.27 to 37.58 N and 108.41 to 110.29 E so field country is CN and the average coordinate is N37.2083 E109.5833 with location range coded 0 because the farthest differences from the average are under two degrees. The basin land use is stated as mostly grassland with some woodland so the dominant habitat type is grassland. The study concerns vegetation NDVI and vegetation cover so the taxon is Plantae. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable because resilience is inferred and no disturbance response window is quantified.

The study developed and used a model and states that it was used for assessing resilience across a wide range of conditions and that it simulates stand replacing fire with fire size and fire free interval as external control parameters. Resilience is defined in the introduction as the ability to avoid qualitative state changes and the analyses operationalize this using extirpation thresholds and persistence across fire rotations. The methods state that equilibrium density was computed during the remaining 1000 years after discarding the first 500 years and that equilibrium density was used as a proxy for fitness and extirpation was defined using a density threshold. The disturbance is fire and it is imposed in the model as circular events that occur as a Poisson process and kill trees within the perimeter which is a pulse event treatment. The quantification aligns with latitude because the key reported resilience related outcome is the extirpation threshold in fire rotation space which is a limit beyond which the original forest composition or persistence cannot be maintained. The target variable used in the core resilience related comparisons is adult tree density at equilibrium and total tree density at equilibrium. The case study region is subalpine forests of Yellowstone National Park Wyoming USA and the model was parameterized for that system even though empirical data were not used in the research. The first author affiliation lists University of Wisconsin Madison in the USA. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated from Yellowstone National Park. The simulation duration for each run is 1500 years which equals 547500 days.

Framework: ecosystem health = vigour + organisation + resilience + services
Justification: The study states it used the Vigour Organisation Resilience Services model to analyse changes in ecosystem health and it defines ecosystem health as derived from the normalised geometric mean of V O R and S. Ecosystem resilience is not measured from recovery dynamics but is assigned from land use because the vegetation recovery coefficient and resistance index are determined based on relevant studies and values associated with the respective urban expansion areas and Table 2 provides resilience values by land use class. Therefore resilience measurement is inferred and quantification is inferred. The resilience target variable is land use because resilience is based on land use categories in Table 2. The work is at landscape level because the resilience values are mapped across land use patches and used in city scale ecological security pattern analysis. The approach includes field observation because land use is visually interpreted based on Landsat 5 7 8 data and NDVI is obtained from Landsat using Google Earth Engine and it includes modeling and simulation because the PLUS model is used to predict urban expansion trends in 2035 and simulate scenarios. The first author affiliation is Chengdu University of Technology in China. The study area is Chengdu in Sichuan Province China and coordinates are given as 102 54 to 104 53 E and 30 05 to 31 26 N so the average is N30.655 E103.892 and the range is within two degrees in both latitude and longitude so location range is 0. Habitat is multi because the land use classes include croplands forests shrublands grasslands water bodies impermeable surfaces and bare land and the resilience proxy is defined across these multiple habitat types. Disturbance fields are NA because measurement is inferred and the study does not quantify resilience as response to a defined disturbance time series.

The study imposed a severe summer drought using rainout shelters from 9 July to 6 September 2010 and also manipulated community structure by removal of subordinate species and partial removal of dominant biomass. Resistance recovery and resilience were calculated from above-ground biomass harvested in early July and early September 2009 2010 and 2011 with biomass separated by species and weighed. Resilience was defined as the ratio of total biomass after the drought year 2011 to total biomass before the drought year 2009 and the paper states that resilience combines resistance and recovery to provide a measure of community stability. Because resilience was quantified directly from the system response in biomass before and after the imposed drought it is observed. The target variable used to quantify resistance recovery and resilience was community above-ground biomass. Biomass was measured across multiple plant species in plots so the level is community and the taxon is Plantae. The experiment was conducted in Swiss Jura grassland at La Frétaz western Switzerland with coordinates 6 34 30 E and 46 50 50 N. The first author affiliation is EPFL in Lausanne Switzerland. The drought was a time bounded event and recovery was assessed after it so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The resilience related observation window spans from the pre drought year 2009 to the post drought year 2011 which is two years or 730 days.

The study calculated six resilience indices using individual tree ring width series and formulas including Resistance = RWDr divided by RWpreDr and Resilience = RWpostDr divided by RWpreDr and Recoveryperiod = Yearrecover minus YearDr. RWpreDr and RWpostDr were defined as mean tree ring width of four years before and after the extreme drought year of 2001 and RWDr was tree ring width in 2001. This uses observed tree ring width time series and an identified extreme drought year so the measurement is observed. No explicit equation framework defining resilience as part of a larger construct is provided because the paper lists indices but does not define a higher level variable including resilience. Quantification includes resistance because Resistance is explicitly computed. Quantification includes recovery degree because Resilience and Recovery are ratios comparing post drought growth to pre drought or drought year growth. Quantification includes recovery time because Recoveryperiod is explicitly computed as years to reach pre drought growth level. The target variable used in resilience calculations is tree ring width which is a growth measure. Indices were calculated for each tree so the level is individual. Sampling used increment cores collected during fieldwork in 2019 and tree ring chronologies over 1980 to 2018 so the approach is field observation. The first author affiliation is Northeast Forestry University Harbin China so institution country is CN. The study area is in Inner Mongolia northern China and sites have coordinates in Table 1 so field country is CN. The three sites are DHB 109.38E 40.75N DQS 111.14E 40.91N and LC 112.29E 40.64N so the average location is N40.77 E110.94 and the farthest deviation sums to 1.12 degrees. The sampled systems are secondary coniferous broad leaved mixed forests so habitat type is forest. The focal species are Pinus tabuliformis Betula platyphylla and Quercus liaotungensis so taxon is Plantae. The disturbance is the extreme drought in 2001 identified from climate data so disturbance type is unmanipulated climatic and it is treated as a discrete year event so the pattern is pulse. The study period for tree ring width chronologies and climate data is 1980 to 2018 so observation duration is 39 years which is 14235 days.

The study imposed prescribed fires because plots were burned every year from 2015 to 2021 during the mid dry season and fire was ensured to be the only factor affecting the vegetation. Resilience was evaluated using the system response measured from field data because the authors counted and identified all grass individuals within permanent 1 m2 subplots before the first fire in 2015 and again after seven prescribed fires in 2022. The focal response variable used in the main resilience statements is grass density because results state seven annual fires doubled grass density from 8 to 17 individuals per m2 and discussion states grass density doubled evidencing the grass layer resilience to repeated fires. This is a before after comparison of state following a disturbance regime rather than a rate or time to return so the quantification aligns with recovery degree. The framework is not explicitly defined as an equation including resilience because the paper states expected to test the resilience sensu Dakos and Kefi 2022 but does not define resilience as a sum of components. The approach is a field experiment because it used permanent plots with prescribed burning treatments at a field site. The institution country is Brazil because the first author affiliation lists Universidade Estadual de Campinas Campinas Brazil. The field country is Brazil because the study site is Estação Ecológica de Santa Bárbara São Paulo Brazil. The location is derived from the site coordinate range 22 degrees 46 minutes 33 seconds to 22 degrees 50 minutes 30 seconds south and 49 degrees 10 minutes 27 seconds to 49 degrees 15 minutes 36 seconds west and reported as the midpoint. The habitat is grassland because plots were grassland classified locally as campo sujo with canopy cover below 5 percent. The taxon is Plantae because the study subjects are grasses. The disturbance pattern is press because annual burns were maintained across the whole 2015 to 2021 period with no recovery interval without fire within the treatment window. Observation duration is from March 2015 to March 2022 which is seven years or 2555 days using 365 days per year.

Framework: engineering resilience = time of complete recovery
Justification: The abstract states that time series Landsat imagery during 1994–2015 were used to observe forest recovery of Wu-Ling fire scars in Taiwan. The introduction states that time of complete recovery tc could be predicted from the trajectory of forest recovery and that engineering resilience here is defined as tc with stable state assumed as the level of pre-disturbed or undisturbed reference area. The methods define NBR and dNBR and then fit time series of NBR to a single exponential equation to estimate tc. The results state that the decay curve model could be used to predict the time of complete recovery tc and Table 5 reports tc in years. Because resilience is defined as tc the quantification is recovery time. The observed variable used for the recovery trajectory is NBR which the paper describes as sensitive to chlorophyll content and water content and is used as a spectral index for forest recovery so the target variable type is ecosystem function. The study area is Wu-Ling farm in central Taiwan and the fire scars are Huan-Shan and Sheng-Guang so the field country is Taiwan. The disturbance is wildfire with historical records of burn dates so it is unmanipulated fire and treated as discrete events. The Landsat scene list spans from 06/11/1994 to 16/11/2015 which is 21 years or 7665 days. The first author affiliation lists National Chung Hsing University in Taichung Taiwan so the institution country is Taiwan. The habitat is described as pine plantation forest with overstory species including Pinus taiwanensis so the habitat type is forest and the taxon is Plantae.

The introduction defines resilience as the capacity of an ecosystem to resist and recover from stress. The methods describe a 5 year field experiment with rainout shelters set up for around 75 days each summer that excluded 100% precipitation and created warmer and drier conditions. Vegetation variables were repeatedly measured including spring plant cover and ANPP across years and treatments. The discussion states that resilience was mainly based on post drought recovery and that spring plant cover following the summer drought was used to estimate plant survival and recovery. The results report an abrupt loss of resilience characterized by a reduction of spring plant cover below 50% and decreased ANPP in rainy years when the extreme water stress index exceeded about 37 mm 10 day−1. A resilience index was calculated as the ratio between plant response under drought versus ambient treatment and significant differences to 1 full recovery were tested. The first author affiliation list includes CEFE Univ Montpellier in France. The site is located at the INRAE La Fage station southern France with coordinates 43 55 N 3 05 E. The study subjects are herbaceous plant communities and the target variable used to characterize resilience loss is spring plant cover.

Framework: resilience indices = resistance + recovery + resilience + relative resilience + recovery period + total growth reduction + average recovery rate
Quantification: Recovery time; Recovery rate; Recovery degree; Resistance
Justification: The study used dendrochronology to reconstruct past growth patterns and calculated resilience indices from standardized ring width series for the 2005 and 2012 droughts. The Methods define resistance as sustaining growth during the stress episode and recovery as the ability to recover after disturbance and resilience as the ability to reach pre disturbance growth levels. The Methods also calculated recovery period as the time in years needed to recover pre drought growth and average recovery rate as the percentage of recovery during the recovery period and total growth reduction as total growth loss during and after the drought event. These indices are computed from observed ring width and basal area increment growth data rather than from proxies. The disturbance is drought identified using SPEI and described as dry years 2005 and 2012 which are discrete events used for pre and post periods. The target variable used for resilience quantification is radial growth from ring width indices at the individual level. Field sampling was conducted at two sites in northeastern Spain and the first author affiliation is Zaragoza Spain. The site coordinates are given for Alcubierre and Valcuerna and the average coordinate is reported. The observation window for growth series used in figures and analyses is 1980 to 2020 which corresponds to 40 years.

The study develops an assessment framework for tourism socio-ecological system resilience and applies an improved TOPSIS model to compute a TSESR value from an index system rather than measuring recovery dynamics of an ecosystem state variable. The framework is explicitly stated as comprising four domains resistance recovery adaptation and renewal. The indicator system in Table 1 uses socio-economic and governance style indicators such as tourism income deposits hotels healthcare beds education and infrastructure along with some ecological indicators such as NDVI habitat quality and PM2.5 which together form the constructed resilience index. Because resilience is evaluated from these proxy indicators and aggregated into a composite index it is inferred rather than observed. The case study covers 14 prefecture level units in Xinjiang China from 2010 to 2020 using multi source statistical and spatial data. The first author affiliation is Xinjiang Institute of Ecology and Geography Chinese Academy of Sciences in Urumqi China. The study area is Xinjiang in northwestern China and Fig. 3 shows the study area location but no coordinates are provided so an approximate central coordinate for Xinjiang is used. The study spans multiple landscapes and mentions deserts lakes grasslands and high mountains so the dominant habitat cannot be reduced to a single IUCN type. No focal organism taxon is assessed because the resilience target is a tourism socio-ecological system index. Disturbances are discussed generally such as drought desertification sandstorms and other hazards but resilience quantification does not measure response to a defined disturbance so disturbance fields are NA.

The study uses data simulated using the aquatic ecosystem model PCLake which allows the generation of critical transitions in response to gradual changes in phosphorus load. The methods state that they ran PCLake for 50-year long eutrophication and oligotrophication scenarios and extracted time series of all model state variables and calculated early-warning indicators on these time series. Early-warning indicators are described as assessing loss of resilience and are linked to critical slowing down characterised by an increased recovery time after a minor perturbation and measured via increasing autocorrelation at lag-1 and variance and density ratio. Because resilience is operationalized through AR1 SD and DR trends in the system time series this is an observed measurement in a model with imposed driver change. No explicit equation style framework defining resilience as a sum of components is provided. The quantification corresponds to recovery rate because AR1 is used as an indicator of critical slowing down and increased recovery time and the analysis quantifies trends in AR1. The example and results focus on chlorophyll-a and other water quality variables and describe chlorophyll-a as a derivative variable reflecting phytoplankton biomass and lake productivity related conditions so the target variable type is ecosystem function. The simulations represent a shallow lake ecosystem and the variables include ecosystem and food web state variables so the level is ecosystem. The approach is modeling and simulation because all data are generated by PCLake runs. The first author affiliation is Netherlands Institute of Ecology in Wageningen so the institution country is NL. The virtual lake is configured with Dutch weather conditions but the data are simulated and no field sampling country or coordinates are given for resilience quantification so field country and location are NA. The system is a lake and thus best matches wetland habitat. The subjects are virtual model compartments rather than real taxa so taxon is virtual. Disturbance includes modelled gradual phosphorus load change as a resource driver and stochastic temperature variation as climatic perturbations. The phosphorus load is linearly increased or decreased over 50 years so the disturbance pattern is press. Daily output spans 50 years so the observation duration is 50 times 365 days.

The study states it was an operational scale experiment with harvesting treatments consisting of increasing levels of dispersed retention including clearcut through 75 percent retention and uncut controls applied in winter 1998 to 1999. It describes this as a pulse experiment in which evidence of subsequent recovery of carabid assemblages was monitored. Ground beetles were sampled with pitfall traps during May through August of 1999 2000 and 2004 which are 1 2 and 5 years postharvest. The response variable analyzed was plot level catch rate of 59 carabid species analyzed as Bray Curtis dissimilarities which is community composition. The methods state that significant differences in pairwise comparisons between harvesting treatments and control stands over time were interpreted as a measure of resistance and potential resilience of ground beetle composition to the effects of harvesting. Resilience is discussed alongside resistance including the key words ecosystem resilience and resistance and the explicit interpretation sentence in methods. The site is located about 90 km northwest of Peace River Alberta Canada with coordinates 56 46 13 N and 118 22 28 W. The first author affiliation is Universite du Quebec a Montreal in Canada. The disturbance is harvesting which is a form of biological resource use and it is manipulated by the experimenters. The observation window spans from the first sampling in 1999 to the last sampling in 2004 which is five years or 1825 days.

The introduction states that the authors analyse grassland stability and resilience over an exceptionally long time series and that changes in composition need to be analysed in time during and after disturbance. The methods describe a long term permanent quadrat plot research with four 3 × 3 m plots inventoried annually over 35 years using Braun Blanquet cover abundance transformed to a decimal scale and used to calculate temporal beta diversity and abundance based dissimilarity. The methods explicitly say local community resilience is measured as the stability of communities over short time steps of annual measures and that annual versus decadal turnover gives a measure for community stabilising mechanisms and that the temporal beta Simpson component gives a measure for resilience. The results and discussion describe recovery language including that functional group composition returned to the same as at the beginning and state this is in terms of recovery to initial conditions. The study also treats resistance in parallel with resilience and Table 1 labels indicators as resistance or resilience under a stability heading and the discussion lists resistance strategies as one mechanism. Disturbances are defined as pulse disturbances of climatic origin extremely dry or wet years and a press disturbance of habitat degradation at the landscape scale and the methods identify a perturbation period in rainfall variability. The field site is near Maastricht at the Belgian Dutch border and meteorological data are from nearby stations and the grassland complex is Thier de Lanaye. The focal organisms are higher plants angiosperms and the habitat is calcareous grassland. The observation window is 35 years which corresponds to 12775 days using 365 days per year.

The study derived transition probabilities from repeated censuses of fixed points in the middle intertidal community of Tatoosh Island Washington USA and used these observational data to parameterize Markov models. Resilience was explicitly assessed as return time because the text states that manipulated models predicted that extinction of several species affects the resilience of the system as assessed by return time and return time was calculated as 1 divided by the subdominant eigenvalue. The framework is none because resilience is not defined as a component of a higher level equation and is only discussed as a response metric alongside community composition. The target variable used for resilience quantification is community composition because the model projects proportions of points in ecological states and compares predicted composition and return time across manipulated matrices. The level is community because the ecological states include multiple species and the analysis concerns community composition and dynamics. The approach includes field observation for the repeated censuses and independent transects and modeling and simulation for the Markov model analyses and stochastic simulations. The first author affiliation is University of Chicago in the United States. The field data were collected on Tatoosh Island Washington USA. No coordinates are provided so the location is coded to Tatoosh Island Washington with approximate coordinates and the study is confined to that site so the range is zero. The habitat is rocky intertidal which fits the coastal category. The subjects include multiple taxa including mussels barnacles algae and birds so the taxon is multi.
